title: HashMap & Set
subtitle: Store things by name with a HashMap, and keep a list with no duplicates using a Set.
goal: Use a HashMap to store key-value pairs (put, get, containsKey, getOrDefault) and a HashSet to store unique values (add, contains), and know when to reach for each one.

blocks.heading: What is a HashMap? 🗂️
blocks.html:
An array stores things by a number (position 0, 1, 2...). A HashMap stores things by a name you pick. Each entry is a key (the name you look it up by) pointing to a value (the thing it holds).

Think of a phone book: you look up a person's name (the key) to find their number (the value). To use one, you import it at the top of your file:

import java.util.HashMap; and import java.util.Map;

blocks.heading: Putting things in and getting them back
blocks.html:
You create a HashMap like this: Map<String, String> book = new HashMap<>();. The two types in the angle brackets are the key type and the value type — here both are String.

Then you use put(key, value) to add an entry and get(key) to look one up. containsKey(key) tells you true or false if a key is there.

blocks.caption: A phone book with names and numbers
blocks.output:
Maya's number: 555-1234
Leo's number: 555-6789
Is Sam in the book? true
Is Zoe in the book? false
blocks.explain: put("Maya", "555-1234") stores the number under the name Maya. get("Maya") looks it back up. containsKey checks if a name exists without crashing — Sam is in the book (true) but Zoe is not (false).

blocks.html: Order is NOT promised! A HashMap does not remember the order you added things in. If you loop over it, names may print in any order. That is why we look things up with get() in these examples — the answer is always the same.

blocks.heading: Counting things with getOrDefault 🔢
blocks.html:
A super common job is counting: how many times does each word appear? The trick is getOrDefault(key, fallback). It returns the value for a key, OR a fallback if the key is missing yet.

So counts.getOrDefault(word, 0) + 1 means: take the count so far (or 0 if it's the first time we've seen it), then add one.

blocks.caption: Counting how often each word shows up
blocks.output:
apple appears 3 times
banana appears 2 times
cherry appears 1 times
blocks.explain: The first time we see a word, getOrDefault returns 0, so we store 1. Every time after, it returns the count so far and we add 1. We print specific get() lookups (not a loop) so the order is always the same.

blocks.html: Looping a HashMap: when you DO want every entry, use keySet() to loop the keys, or entrySet() to loop key-and-value pairs together. Just remember the order is not guaranteed — sort the keys first if you need a tidy order.

blocks.heading: What is a HashSet? 🎯
blocks.html:
A HashSet is like a bag that only keeps unique things — it throws away duplicates automatically. There are no keys and values, just a collection of values where each one appears at most once.

Import it with import java.util.HashSet; and import java.util.Set;. Use add(value) to put something in and contains(value) to check if it's there. Adding the same thing twice does nothing the second time.

blocks.caption: Counting unique visitors (duplicates ignored)
blocks.output:
Unique visitors: 3
Did Maya visit? true
Did Zoe visit? false
blocks.explain: We added 5 names but Maya and Leo were repeated, so the set keeps only 3 unique ones. size() counts them. contains() checks membership: Maya is in (true), Zoe was never added (false).

blocks.heading: When do I use which? 🤔
blocks.html:
Pick a HashMap when each thing needs an attached value — a name to a number, a word to its count, a student to their grade. Pick a HashSet when you only care whether something is in the group and want no duplicates — a list of unique visitors, tags already used, or seats already taken.

Short version: HashMap = lookup table (key gives you a value). HashSet = guest list (just "is it in or not?").

blocks.question: In a HashMap, what does get("Maya") return?
options: The value stored under the key "Maya"
All the keys in the map
blocks.explain: get(key) looks up the value paired with that key. If we stored phoneBook.put("Maya", "555-1234"), then get("Maya") returns "555-1234".

blocks.question: You add "Leo" to a HashSet twice. How many times is "Leo" stored?
options: Once — a Set keeps only unique values
blocks.explain: A HashSet ignores duplicates. Adding "Leo" a second time does nothing, so it is stored only once.

blocks.question: Why do these lessons print get() lookups instead of looping over the HashMap?
options: Looping is not allowed in Java
A HashMap's iteration order is not guaranteed, so looking up specific keys keeps the output the same every time
You cannot loop over a HashMap at all
blocks.explain: HashMap and HashSet do not promise any order when you loop them. Printing specific get() results gives the same output on every run.

blocks.title: Build a pet age book
blocks.instructions: Add the cat to the HashMap with the key "cat" and the value "9", so both lines print. Press Check.
blocks.solutionOutput:
The dog is 12
The cat is 9
blocks.passMsg: Nice — your map now holds both pets, looked up by name!
blocks.failMsg: Add the line ages.put("cat", "9"); below the dog line so the cat has a value too.

blocks.title: Count unique numbers
blocks.instructions: We add 5, 5, 8, 5, 2 to a HashSet. Finish the print line to show how many UNIQUE numbers it holds using numbers.size(). Press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Spot on — the duplicates of 5 only count once, so the answer is 3!
blocks.failMsg: Use numbers.size() to count the unique values. The repeated 5s only count once.

blocks.title: HashMap problems
blocks.intro: HashMap turns O(n²) problems into O(n).

challenge.title: Vote counter with a map and a set
challenge.instructions: Loop over the votes array. For each vote, bump its count in the HashMap with getOrDefault(vote, 0) + 1, and add it to the HashSet. Then the prints will show how many different foods there are and the count for each. Press Check.
challenge.solutionOutput:
Different foods: 2
pizza votes: 3
tacos votes: 2
challenge.hint: Inside the loop write two lines: tally.put(vote, tally.getOrDefault(vote, 0) + 1); and foods.add(vote);
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 You used a HashMap to count and a HashSet to find unique items — two of the most useful tools in all of programming. Day 18 complete!
challenge.failMsg: In the loop, count with tally.put(vote, tally.getOrDefault(vote, 0) + 1); and track unique foods with foods.add(vote);
